request.question: When did Limp Bizkit become a rap rock band?

title: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all
Limp Bizkit
Icon (Limp Bizkit album)
Eat You Alive
Ready to Go (Limp Bizkit song)
Results May Vary
Limp Bizkit discography
Significant Other
Stampede of the Disco Elephants
Home Sweet Home/Bittersweet Symphony
sentences: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.
Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.
Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.
Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).
Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.
Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.
The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.
Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".
"Eat You Alive" is a song by the band Limp Bizkit.
It was released in September 2003 as a single from their fourth studio album "Results May Vary" (2003).
The song was written by Fred Durst, John Otto, Sam Rivers and Mike Smith, and is Limp Bizkit's first single without Wes Borland, who had left the band in 2001.
"Ready to Go" is a song by the American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
The single features rapper and then label-mate Lil Wayne and is produced by Polow Da Don.
The single is Limp Bizkit's first release for Cash Money Records after their departure from Interscope in 2011.
The song was released in March 2013 as a free download on the band's website and, on April 16, as a digital single on iTunes and Amazon.
Results May Vary is the fourth studio album by the American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released on September 23, 2003, it is the band's only release under the sole-leadership of vocalist Fred Durst after the temporary departure of guitarist Wes Borland, who left the band in 2001.

Snot guitarist Mike Smith was initially brought in to replace Borland, although the band's falling-out with Smith later lead to his departure, with much of the material recorded with him being discarded from the final release.
Durst and a number of guests ended up handling the majority of the album's guitar work.

The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.
Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.
The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.
"Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.
The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.
Stampede of the Disco Elephants is the upcoming sixth studio album by American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
It is set to be Limp Bizkit's first album to be released since DJ Lethal's departure in May 2012.
After the original line-up reunited in 2009, "Gold Cobra" was released in 2011.
The band left Interscope and signed up with Cash Money Records in February 2012.
The first single "Lightz" was released on October 26, 2012.
After many delays, the second single for the album, "Ready to Go", featuring label mate Lil Wayne was released on April 16, 2013, to positive reviews.
It is the first album since "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)" to be produced by Ross Robinson.
"Home Sweet Home / Bittersweet Symphony", also known as "Bittersweet Home" is a song by American rock group Limp Bizkit.
It was released in December 2005 as the only single from the compilation album "Greatest Hitz".
The song is a medley of "Home Sweet Home" by Mötley Crüe and "Bitter Sweet Symphony" by The Verve.

It is, along with the songs "Why" and "Lean On Me", the only new content on the "Greatest Hitz" compilation, and is the third cover song Limp Bizkit has released on an album (the others being The Who's "Behind Blue Eyes", and George Michael's "Faith").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

hop1_docs: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all | Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.  Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.

Significant Other | Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.  "Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.  The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.

Limp Bizkit | Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.  Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).  Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.  Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.  The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.

Released in 2005, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water" and "Results May Vary".

Icon (Limp Bizkit album) | Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Collected (Limp Bizkit album) | Collected is the third compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2008, it a retrospective compilation album from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Limp Bizkit discography | The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.  Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.  The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [3] "formed in 1994" (passage 3: "formed in 1994").
  - [7] "formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida." (passage 7: "formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida").
  - [3] "Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band" (passage 3, paraphrase).
  - [1] Debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Y'all" released July 1, 1997 (passage 1: "Released on July 1, 1997").
  - [1] Debut album described as by "American rap rock band Limp Bizkit" (passage 1, paraphrase).
  - [2] Second album "Significant Other" released in 1999 and described as by "American rap rock band" (passage 2, paraphrase).
  - [7] Discography page labels them "nu metal band" and says they "formed in 1995" (passage 7, paraphrase).

- Bridge entity and ambiguous/missing facts:
  - Bridge entity: Limp Bizkit (the band).
  - Ambiguities/missing facts preventing answer:
    - Formation year conflict: passages list both 1994 [3] and 1995 [7].
    - No passage states a specific date or moment "when they became a rap rock band" (i.e., when rap-rock elements were adopted or when they were first described as rap rock).
    - Genre label inconsistency: passages call them "rap rock" [1–3] and "nu metal" [7]; unclear which label applies at what time.

- Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve gaps:
  1. Fetch the full "Limp Bizkit" main page (or band infobox and "History"/"Formation"/"Early years" sections). Extract: "Years active"/formation year, genre(s) listed in infobox, and timeline text about early style/when rap-rock elements began (look for dates, member changes such as DJ Lethal joining).
  2. Fetch "Three Dollar Bill, Y'all" page (full). Extract: "Background"/"Recording"/"Composition" sections and any notes on the band's musical style at that time (evidence that rap-rock sound was present by the 1997 debut).
  3. Fetch "DJ Lethal" and "Wes Borland" pages or band-member bios. Extract: dates they joined Limp Bizkit and descriptions of contributions (scratching, hip hop elements) to identify when rap elements entered the band's sound.
  4. Fetch reliable secondary sources (e.g., band interviews, music press timeline) or the "Limp Bizkit discography" page. Extract: earliest contemporary descriptions calling them "rap rock"/"rap-metal", and dates of first releases/performances that showcase rap-rock style.

No final answer provided: the supplied passages are inconsistent on formation year and contain no explicit statement of when the band adopted a rap-rock identity. Fetch the targets above to resolve the question.

response.summary_2: Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock (also described as nu metal) band formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida. Limp Bizkit released their debut album Three Dollar Bill, Y'all on July 1, 1997.
response.answer: The summaries do not give a specific date when they "became" rap rock. They are described as formed in 1995 and are called a rap rock band (and their debut album, released July 1, 1997, is described that way), so at least by 1997 they were described as rap rock, but the exact moment is not provided in the summaries.
